Transcrição Completa: 68 Risk Mitigation Strategies At Scale 11 16 24
[00:00] First of all, pretty funny. Dicky requested a weekly group call. Didn't even show up to the weekly group call. Shout out to you, Dicki. And uh we got a request from him this week on risk mitigation strategies. Uh to be clear, and this is actually very coincidental. If I scroll up a little bit, there's actually a video I filmed on Thursday about this topic for like one-third of the video, which drops later this afternoon, but of course, it won't cover nearly the detail in which I'll cover here today. So, I thought that was funny.
[00:35] Um, okay. First of all, all risk mitigation strategies, just as you saw above there, all come down to two things. Number one, inner peace. And number two, how can I get fucked. These two things are the main ways that we can handle almost all risks that are otherwise going to fuck us or ruin our inner peace.
[01:02] Okay, let me start by saying this as a few examples. When you start spending some serious money, okay, the things that can take you down even for like a day or 72 hours, like even if it's just a few days, the problems get exacerbated by a tremendous amount when you're at large levels of spend in comparison to these like tiny levels of spend when you're first starting off and testing things.
[01:28] A problem that exists at like 10K a month in ad spend that takes you down for like three days might result in you missing out on like a few thousand you know it's nothing too substantial versus okay if I'm spending like 10k 15k or like 20k plus a day it's like dude one fucking day of going down could be the difference of tens of thousands or potentially even hundreds of thousands of dollars in revenue.
[01:55] So, you can't have down days. And I don't mean days where the performance isn't good. I mean like something literally is preventing you from spending money at all. Okay?
[02:06] When I talk about inner peace, I know it kind of sounds funny, but like honestly, I I'm not even like trying to be funny with it. I'm like very serious about it. When I I give you a perfect example with um an account I had in like I think it was like 2019 2020, we were spending upwards of like $1.2 million a month on ad spend. and we were flipping it into like $3 4 million a month.
[02:33] Can't talk about who the client is, by the way. I actually am under NDA for that one. But to be clear, at this level of spend, okay, we're pumping some serious fucking cash out. Like, we're at like 30 40k a day, give or take the day, and where we're at in the scale, okay? Some days even larger than that.
[02:53] And I'll never forget this, like I felt I felt like genuinely fucked for like not doing this risk mitigation strategy for this individual. So, one day I wake up and the ad account's down and I already had a few other ad accounts that were in motion and that we were spending in. I had a few other pixels that like we actively had conditioned and like already seasoned up.
[03:16] And the difference was is this main account was responsible for like so much of the daily spend that when I tried to move like 30 to 40k a day in spend to these other accounts, those other accounts had only been spending like at most like $2 to $5,000 a day. And unbeknownst to me, those other accounts had $5,000 a day limits, which is very common.
[03:39] By the way, when you start spending serious money, you generally have to hit up Facebook and ask them, "Hey, I need to spend more money. Is it possible for you to increase my daily limit?" And they'll happily do it. But here's the worst part. When I woke up to that issue of the account being down and needing to mitigate that spend going elsewhere, it was a weekend. It was like a Saturday afternoon.
[04:02] Who here has had an issue with Facebook on Saturday afternoon? How good are they at solving problems on weekends? They fucking suck at doing anything on the weekend. They don't they don't even work on the weekend. They're like typical nine-to-fives. Okay, point being.
[04:17] Well, I tried to move that spend over and I wasn't able to. I was only I was only able to spend about 10k a day between the two other backup ad accounts I had created in a frenzy of trying to get additional spend off because now I'm spending 25% of what I'm supposed to for a full Saturday and Sunday and potentially through like Monday and Tuesday as well.
[04:40] Well, now I'm in a position where I have to hit up Facebook on Monday and I have to wait for them to respond and I have to wait for them to actually raise it up on that Saturday afternoon because I recognized that was going to be an issue. I started creating new ad accounts within that business manager. All those had limits, too. And they weren't even close to $5,000 a day.
[05:02] So, again, something I could have easily predicted, something I could have been in front of, I didn't. And that entire weekend was fucked. I had no inner peace that entire weekend because I'm spending 25% of what I'm supposed to. And in that particular month, instead of doing three to four million dollars, we ended up being down a total of like five to six days because it took Facebook that long from like Monday through like Wednesday to actually raise the cap on the backup accounts.
[05:32] And it also took our connect that we could pay off to get the main account back up until Friday of that week to get it back online. that entire week, okay, that 5 to six days of being down, without exaggeration, lost the business a grand total of about $700,000 in revenue because I did not mitigate risk. I didn't think ahead of time about how I was going to get fucked. I didn't think ahead of time about things that could bring me inner peace.
[06:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Risk Mitigation Categories
NÚMEROS: 3 categorias principais
VISUAL: Três colunas organizadas: "Collecting Money", "Collecting Data", "Stability" - layout limpo com títulos em negrito, estrutura de framework
[06:05] Okay, so risk mitigation strategies come from a few things. Okay, uh I I generally think that they're broke down into categories. Okay. So we have collecting money, collecting data and stability. These are the three categories I always think with when I'm looking at risk mitigation.
[06:23] Okay. So let me give you some examples under collecting money. First, payment processors. I said this at the mastermind because we had our first sponsor with a payment processor company called WOW. And dude, when these guys hit me up and they wanted to sponsor, I I actually thought to myself, I'm like, this is probably the first time ever I'd actually consider a sponsorship from somebody because it's fully aligned to like what I do in every deal anyway.
[06:50] And a lot of the other payment processors out there are fucked. Like they're not as loose as what fucking is in comparison. So anyway, long story short, put in the chat how many of you have a single payment processor? Like you just use Stripe or like you just have like one. Put in the chat how many you have. but a number.
[07:10] Yeah, good. Yeah, you guys were at the mastermind. Smart people. [laughter] Thank god you guys are all putting more than a number one. Yeah, good. Okay, now here's the second question. How many of you are actually running good money through all of these payment processors that you just listed? You have almost all of you listed two. Are you actually running a good chunk of revenue through the backup ones or are you just letting them sit there idle?
[07:36] Like keep in mind, I'll give you a perfect example again. Let's use that same company I just talked about. Okay, so they're doing upwards of three to $4 million a month. Okay, if let's just say it's three million. Okay, we take $3 million and we divide it by 30 days in the month. That's an average of a 100k a day.
[07:57] And they're usually accepting credit cards, by the way. If the main payment processor goes down and let's just say I have an account with a backup payment processor, but I never use it, but like I'm approved to use it and then randomly I just start trying to run 100K a day through it. Somebody tell me, do you think they'll let you do that? Fucking no.
[08:18] No, they're going to shut you down. They're going to hold all that money in reserve. They're probably going to force refund it and then they're going to hit you up and do a compliance audit. They're going to be like, "What are you doing?" Like, give us all these details about your business.
[08:32] Anybody ever had that happen with PayPal where they hit you up and they're like, "What are you doing?" Like, and then they hold your money for like six fucking months. PayPal's the devil. Don't ever use PayPal. Okay, that's another good example of a risk mitigation strategy when it comes to collecting money.
[08:48] How many of you have heard horror stories of PayPal? Like, I'm talking like literal horror stories. Okay, I'll give you a good one for myself. I did 90 93k in a day back in 2017 when I first launched my agency honor course, dude. They held $93,000 for I still haven't seen that $93,000. I didn't do fucking wrong. Collected money, but I did it out of nowhere and I did it under business.
[09:18] But again, they just kept all my money, okay? It's like we have to also find payment processors that we can condition up, okay? That we run some money through. They can't just be sitting there idly by. Okay?
[09:32] collecting money can also come down to wires, which really comes down to the banks. Okay, if you guys were there for the uh what was it, the Q4 inner circle mastermind that just happened in October, we did a no phones meeting with uh with Ryan for his talk about doing $55 million and then going bankrupt.
[09:54] Okay. And one thing that he talked about with his banks, you know, well before anything was problematic was he was running so much money through different banks that the banks did the same thing that payment processors would do like PayPal. They would just freeze his assets. They would freeze the money in his accounts and they'd make him do these like audits to essentially find out what he's doing in his business.
[10:19] And then as a result of that, dude, hundreds [snorts] of thousands or in some instances millions of dollars. Like I remember he was in a really good position. There was nothing wrong probably like halfway through his whole story that he told us about and Bank of America just shut him down. Bank of America held his money. He had to sue Bank of America and that caused a lot of stress, a lot of problems and it again it stole his inner peace and forced him into a federal lawsuit with Bank of America.
[10:50] Okay. again. Put in the chat how many bank accounts do you got with different banks. Okay, put the number in the chat. 3 2 4 1. Shame on you, Max. You better up your banks. Three. Three. Good. Two. Okay.
[11:07] So, be clear again. It's like same kind of logic I just provided with the payment processors. you all of a sudden start stuffing tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of dollars in a day into a bank account out of nowhere, what are they going to do? You think they're going to be cool? No. You got to condition it up. You got to condition it up. Okay? You have to actually divest away from just being all-in on one thing, even if that one thing's working fine and there's no issues. Because again, that's how we can prevent getting fucked. That's how we can maintain inner peace. You see what I'm saying?
[11:43] Okay. To be clear, collecting money is generally as simple really as just your payment processors and your banks. There's not usually much else to it. Okay? There's not additional things I generally list under this category that are like primary drivers. So, I'll move on to the collecting data category.
[12:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Collecting Data - Pixels
NÚMEROS: 3 business managers, 3 ad accounts cada = 9 ad accounts totais
VISUAL: Estrutura hierárquica mostrando "Multiple of Threes" - três business managers com três ad accounts dentro de cada um, representação visual de diversificação de dados
[12:02] Okay? This one is really one main thing that I care about more than anything else, which is pixels. Okay? Every single time that we come into an account and they want to start spending some serious money, we work in what we refer to as the multiple of threes. Okay. I want three business managers. I want three ad accounts inside of each one of those. So I want three separate ad accounts in each business manager.
[12:28] Okay, it's a demand. So I have a total in this case of nine ad accounts and three business managers. And again, if you're spending like a couple hundred dollars a day, dude, sure you might think that you don't need this, but it's like again, when you start scaling up, even if you get to like 30k or 100k in a month in ad spend, you know, that's a good amount of data that you're going to lose in the instance that you ever get fucked.
[12:54] Okay, that's a good amount of data you're going to lose that you spent to condition the pixel. I remember way back in the day when I worked at Grant's office and I had quit my job and I had went out in the world and I just started talking about what I did at Grant's office. Okay, I was very vocal about it and I'd spoke at different events. I had been invited onto podcasts. I didn't even have to pay for press.
[13:18] People were people were coming up to me and hitting me up and writing press about it and they were like, "Oh, the marketer behind Grant Cardone, you know, and I remember Grant I I had gotten one of my mentors at the time who hit me up and he said, "Hey, like I heard through the grapevine Grant's about to sue you." And I was like, "Whoa, whoa, whoa." Like, "What do you mean?"
[13:40] So he I go, "What do I do?" He goes, "I'd call Grant." So I I pick up my phone, call Grant's cell. He answers. Man, this feeling in my chest is like insane for how it felt. [laughter] Okay. And I'm like, Grant, I'm just Look, first of all, I wouldn't be anywhere remotely close to who I am today without you. You're a mentor to me. I have nothing but like the literal utmost respect for you, and I praise you as much as I possibly can, and I try to flow you power. Why in the world am I hearing that you want to sue me?
[14:14] And he was like, "Well, to be clear, you're just using my name and I don't make any money when you use my name. That's really all it comes down to." He's like, "Every time you use my name, you got to remember like Jeremy, you were spending my money to help build my name and make me money. You know, personally how much money, as an example, just while you were managing it was spent to build my name."
[14:38] And I was like, "Yeah, dude. No, that actually makes a lot of sense. And I'm really sorry about that. I didn't think about it at all. You know, I'm like 21 years old. I'm a fucking idiot. Uh, please forgive me. I'll hit everybody up and try to take it down. Don't sue me. I don't want to beef with a billionaire. Okay. And he was like, "Yeah, yeah, all good."
[14:58] You know, and in that moment, I realized a very valuable lesson, which is again, just how Grant looks at it is how you should look at your spend going towards buying this data. It's not just obviously building your brand and getting your business out there. It's like a part of that spend for like what you're buying, right? It's not like you get a black and white sheet that says, "Here's the offer for when you spend money on Facebook ads for what comes with it."
[15:25] You have to be mindful of what you're buying when you spend ad dollars. Okay? What are you buying? Just in this particular case, when it comes to collecting data, you're buying qualified people who hit your pixel and give you a tremendous competitive advantage.
[15:40] Who here has started an ad account with a brand new pixel and gotten absolutely fucking hosed recently on how terrible the results are initially? Raise your hand. Unmute yourself. Throw it in the chat. Put a yes or no. Who's gotten fucked when you launch a new ad account and have no data on that pixel? How terrible of an experience is that? You see, bad.
[16:03] It's like, yeah, it sucks. But to be clear now, think of the businesses that have already spent like a million dollars on ads. They already have a seasoned pixel. Okay? How big of a comm really think about it. How much of a competitive advantage is that? Somebody else tries to start your exact business, but they do it with a brand new pixel. Dude, that's a huge competitive advantage. That's a literal moat in business.
[16:28] If I have a client who's spent a couple hundred,000 for a couple months in a row and they have like infinite people that have hit that pixel that have been highly qualified and so therefore as a result of that the pixel knows who to go after next with a tremendously higher probability comparatively to a new pixel. Dude, that's a huge competitive advantage, is it not?
[16:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Pixel Diversification Strategy
NÚMEROS: 3 pixels totais, 1 pixel por business manager, todos instalados em cada website
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando três business managers separados, cada um com seu próprio pixel único, todos instalados simultaneamente em todos os websites - representação de redundância e proteção de dados
[16:52] Okay, so to be clear, here's what else we do in order to make sure that our pixels from a collecting data perspective are also well diversified. So remember, if I have three separate business managers on a business manager level, now they typically refer to pixels as data sets. Obviously, we all know them as a pixel. Anyway, point being, we go into the business manager, we go to the data set settings, and we create a pixel per business manager. We place all three pixels on every website that we run traffic to, no matter what. Okay?
[17:27] That way, the standard event that we're optimizing our campaigns for that the qualified people are hitting and sending back to the pixel is not just on a singular pixel within one business manager, okay? It's diversified between three separate business managers with three unique pixels. Okay?
[17:46] Why? Well, if one business manager goes down, that pixel goes down with it. And yes, we have connects here that we can pay off to get the business manager back. That's not the point. The point is is that if you're spending tens of thousands of dollars a day or even thousands of dollars a day and it takes the entire week, like take our connect Stephen as an example. Stephen will not work on the weekends, okay?
[18:11] He has a lot of people that'll hit him up on the weekend to do stuff. He gets backed up on Monday. Your business manager might be down Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, and then it finally comes back online. That's four full days out of a 30-day month that you're down. You get what I'm saying? And if you take four divided by 30, dude, that's 13% of the month that you were down because it happened on a weekend and you weren't able to immediately switch to another pixel in another business manager in one of the three bit ad accounts within those other business managers. You see what I'm saying?
[18:45] Okay, to be clear, I want to make sure that I take a moment just answer any questions on this if there's any like little nuances that you guys need to understand before I continue to stability because there's a lot of stuff under the stability column. Does anybody have any questions on those two so far?
[19:02] PARTICIPANT: Yeah, if you put multiple pixels on a page, that won't fuck anything up, right?
[19:07] No, you should be fine. Uh, to be clear, you can either just raw dog it and put the pixel literally into the header code itself. You can also use the Google Tag Manager for that. And generally, you have less errors that will occur if you do use the Google Tag Manager. And the page typically loads faster as a result since you're essentially putting three pieces of JavaScript on the page if you do it raw dog style.
[19:32] PARTICIPANT: Thank you.
[19:33] Other questions?
[19:35] PARTICIPANT (ANDREW): Andrew, you unmuted yourself. Do you have a question? hear me? Uh, is that three Facebook pixels? Like, would you add a Hyru pixel in there?
[19:46] Uh, to be clear, Hyros doesn't have an uh, like you don't have to mitigate the risk of Hyro to be clear. Like, your Hyros account's not going to get banned. You know what I mean? So, to be clear, yes, it's three separate Facebook pixels and then you would technically have a fourth if you're using the Hyros pixel as well.
[20:04] PARTICIPANT: Got it.
[20:05] To be clear, Hyros isn't a pixel on its own. Hyros is a pixel that you create within Facebook. So it is associated to a specific business manager just to disclose like the technical aspect of that. Um so Hyros doesn't hold the data. The data is still held within the business manager itself. Um it's just named the Hyros pixel and Hyros is pushing data to that specific pixel that's been created. So it still runs under the same risk as everything else I just talked about related to uh standard pixels.
[20:35] PARTICIPANT (JOLIE): Hi Jeremy, I have a I have a quick question.
[20:38] What's up Jolie?
[20:39] JOLIE: Yeah. So I was thinking I was wondering what are the best practices when it comes to creating an additional Facebook personal profile if the goal is ultimately to attach that profile to a business manager because I've tried creating like a backup account for me but it got shut down like after one hour and so I was just wondering what's the best way to do it.
[21:01] Yeah, Facebook does try to prevent against impersonation. So, generally what you do, like I'll give you a good example, like I have I have Facebook profiles for my three dogs and Facebook doesn't care about that. So, to be clear, it's like I got Papito, Lola, and Oreo as my three backup profiles on Facebook. And I just post dog photos occasionally on them. That's that's how I get around that.
[21:30] So, I technically have three backup profiles all connected to my dog. And then, uh vice versa, in my case, I have I have my fiance as well that I can access um and connect her accounts to any business managers if I needed to access it because we're on the same Wi-Fi. You know, sometimes we're on the same devices. Generally not on the same devices to be clear, but regardless, like we're on the same Wi-Fi and she's accessing Facebook at a high frequency as well.
[21:58] So, point being, um if you're just by yourself, like literally just make up the fact that you have an animal and just go grab some animal photos off the internet or like use AI to create some animal photos. And uh pet profiles are typically the best, believe it or not. people I don't know why are under a much higher scrutiny level in comparison to pets. That's why I say pets is a better option. Does that make sense?
[22:24] JOLIE: Yeah, it is possible. I've noticed that there's also sometime that they ask you to verify your identity and you have to upload like your driving license or your passport. Does happen as well when you have like an animal profile? Uh like how does that look like?
[22:40] Yeah, good question. So, that's actually why I like the animal profiles a lot. Um, to be clear, every time I've added them into a business manager as a backup profile, I'll just go into their profile and occasionally like make some ad changes or do something. As long as you have no political affiliations with those profiles and you're not in an account that's ever been disapproved for political profiles, I've noticed that ID verification has gone down tremendously.
[23:08] It was a big issue like early part of last year and I'd also say like through the 2020 election, it was a big issue. I just haven't seen it be problematic or I haven't had to to to do that yet. To be clear, point I'm attempting to make is if that did come up, I'd obviously be fucked from the pet profiles. Thus, why I also say I have my fiance's backup profile, which obviously I I could verify as an ID.
[23:34] Um, for me as well, I also have plenty of staff and those staff we could also do the same thing with where, you know, I just access their profiles because they've also all been to my homes and on my IP addresses. That's the key is like if you're going to use somebody else's backup profile that like is a real person that does have a real ID, then long story short, you just ideally need to make sure that you either go over to their house at some point or they some at some point come over to your house um and you access uh IP and even if you can't get on the same IP address like maybe you guys are in like different parts of the world.
[24:11] Um point being you need to interact frequently with your profiles. That way they also see that you guys are connected. you need to essentially just create a bridge between your profile and theirs. Ideally with hardware and IPs that's the most ideal scenario but again it's even if it's just interaction that can also create a lot of stability in that instance. Does that make sense?
[24:34] JOLIE: Absolutely. Thank you so much.
[24:36] Okay. So any other questions on what I've talked about so far?
[24:41] All right. So yeah, we'll start off with the stability column then with uh what we just talked about, which is personal ad access, which is really just individual profiles. I'm not going to reiterate everything I just did because I pretty much just explained it. Uh but I will just lightly summarize it. Again, I have profiles for each one of my animals. Um I have my fiance's backup profile. I have staff backup profiles.
[25:06] Um, I do attempt to ensure that we all are able to operate on a on the same IP with staff. Obviously, they're typically not at your house or potentially they're at your office or whatever occasionally, but ideally you can get people to come to your home and access Facebook and Instagram from your IP address if you do ever want to use them as a backup profile.
[25:28] um that and ideally even further than that, the same hardware device, like if you can get them to log in on that laptop you've been logging in and accessing Facebook on, that also helps a lot. Um to Jolie's point, the only risk of this is unfortunately, you know, again, if they try to ID a pet profile, like you're pretty fucked. That's why you need people as well as backups. But I haven't had any issues with the pet profiles yet. I think it's kind of a pro tip just based on how Facebook currently is.
[25:56] They've been, you guys know how it goes, like if you've been advertising for a long time, sometimes they're strict, sometimes they're not strict. Right now, we're in a not strict period. So, you know, it's working relatively fine.
[26:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Stability - Funnels & Domain Risk
NÚMEROS: $9K/dia em spend, perda de centenas de milhares em 7-8 dias
VISUAL: Caso de estudo mostrando impacto de domínio bloqueado - representação de funis duplicados em domínios diferentes, conceito de "one in the chamber" (munição pronta), metáfora militar de preparação
[26:10] Um, okay. Funnels. So, just the other day, we had an issue. I guess you could also kind of this this this falls under the pixel. Uh we had a client, it's a consulting client where it's kind of a joint lesson that falls under both of these categories. They woke up to an email that said, "You violated data terms from this specific domain sending data back to the pixel. We're no longer going to accept data from that domain."
[26:37] What's that mean? Well, it means the pixel's still working. It means the pixel still has all the data that it's had on it. But unfortunately, it means that that domain will no longer send data back to the pixel, which means all standard events from that point forward do not get sent back to the pixel. Okay?
[26:56] So all the campaigns that were running and this this account in particular was at like upwards of 9K a day in spend at this point. Just all had no data at all coming into it from the time that that email was sent for several days. Unfortunately, as the consultant on this account, I didn't hear about this issue for days later. Okay.
[27:18] Um, this puts you in a reactive position. Okay, again, it strips you of inner peace and it starts to fuck you dramatically, especially again when you're at 9K a day in spend. Um, this resulted in, without exaggeration, a good couple hundred grand in revenue lost because this problem was exacerbated for a total of like seven to eight days. Okay, you have to have multiple duplicates of the exact same funnels you're running traffic to. now on different domains. Okay?
[27:50] Ideally, you have all of your pixels on these domains as well. You don't have to run a single ounce of traffic to these backup domains or these backup funnels. They're just sitting there idally by. Okay? Picture like the military. Let's use the example that the United States government needs to go strike somebody. Like let's take the Hooies the other day bombing one of our boats. Okay.
[28:14] Is it better to already have the ammo on the boat to bomb the fucking out of the Houthies or is it better to get the ammo after we've already been bombed and get that ammo to the boat? What seems like the faster response time? Obviously already having the fucking ammo in the guns, you know, in those big ass rocket launchers. they've got on these boats, you know, these cannons they've got.
[28:37] It's like not only having the ammo on the boat, but literally having the ammo in the cannon, like one in the chamber, locked and loaded, ready to fire. We don't want the ammo like offshore that we then have to transport to the boat and then load into the weapon. That's what creates the downtime. You see, it's like again from a from a risk mitigation perspective to maintain inner peace, I need duplicates of the funnels I'm already running traffic to on a backup domain and I need to have everything already set up. That's one in the chamber right there. You see what I'm saying?
[29:12] Everybody put a one in the chat if you understand what I just said. Again, one one's in the chat if you understand what I just said. Okay? So, to be clear, how often are you gonna get hit up by Facebook about your domain no longer sending data back to your pixel? Pretty fucking rare, you know? It's not nec again, how often are the fucking hooies going to try to hit a US Navy vessel, you know? It's like that's probably the first time in history that's ever fucking happened, you know?
[29:43] Same thing with this account we're talking about. This company had never had an instance where their domain just randomly got told, "Hey, you're not you're not allowed to send data back to the pixel anymore." How good would it feel comparatively to being down for 7 to 8 days just to be able to instantly duplicate your campaigns, swap the domain, and you're good already? How good would that sound in comparison? See what I'm saying?
[30:09] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Insurance Analogy - Risk Mitigation Philosophy
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação entre seguros tradicionais (vida, carro) e seguros de negócio digital - conceito de que não existe seguro para contas de anúncio, então você precisa criar o seu próprio
[30:11] This is the point I'm trying to make with risk mitigation. It's like, yeah, your payment processor ideally isn't just going to shut you down. You know, your bank's ideally not just going to hit you up and be like, "Hey, sorry. Like, you're under compliance review. Like, we're not doing business with you anymore. We're going to stroke you a cashier's check for whatever balance you have in like 90 days from now." Yeah. Ideally, your pixel is just going to keep working and not get like banned.
[30:35] You could get a pixel banned. You know, we can't pay off the Steven guy to turn back on a pixel, but that can happen. We ideally just have two other pixels we can we can rotate to. Ideally, your personal ad access doesn't go down. Ideally, we don't need to pivot a funnel, but again, that's not the point.
[30:51] It's like life insurance. Yeah, I don't want to fucking die, but like guess what? If I do die, all my heirs become multi-millionaires overnight. You see what I'm saying? It's like, that's the point. It's like insurance, you know? I don't expect to get in a fucking car accident, but if I do, I got car insurance to pay for it. You see what I'm saying?
[31:11] That's what you're doing here. Just answer this question in the chat real quick with a simple yes or no. Is there such a thing as insurance for any of this within your advertising accounts? Can you pay Facebook insurance to do all of this shit for you? Can you pay your bank can you pay your payment processor for insurance? Yes or no? No.
[31:32] So what does that mean? That means we got to spend the little bit of time it takes to do this tedious fucking to create our own insurance policies. Does that make sense? And again, you want to think with everything that could both fuck you and strip you of your inner peace. That's all you're trying to think with when you go through these categories. Okay?
[31:53] Everybody might have a little different thing. It's like obviously this comes down. Let me give you a good example with the with a people category. How many of you have one closer that absolutely fucking crushes it and all your other closers are mediocre? Put a yes in the chat if that's you. You got one closer. It might be you, by the way. You might be your one closer that fucking sweeps and the rest of your team's mediocre as fuck or you got nobody.
[32:20] How do you feel? What happens if that closer gets pneumonia, you know? Or what happens if that closer's mom dies? What happens to your revenue as a result of that? What do you think's going to happen? Yeah, you're fucked. So, you just sit idally by instead of realizing like it feels it feels better to sedate.
[32:41] Okay, this is what most people do. They do two things that fuck them more than anything else, which then forces them into a position of reactivity. They ignore and/or they sedate. Okay. Feels a lot better to just think nothing bad's going to happen. Feels a lot better to just like drink your way through problems or some shit you know, whatever you use for sedation. Okay.
[33:06] My point being, you actually want to get rid of it. You want to bring yourself true inner peace. You want to avoid getting fucked. You want to avoid being put into a reactive position as you're trying to scale to million-dollar months. Yeah. Me too. From that perspective, ignoring things and sedating will never ever be the resolution. This is the worst possible set of conditions that we could be in when we're trying to mitigate risk. Okay?
[33:33] I'd rather you be hyper aware and almost like paranoid about the things that could fuck you for a short duration of time while you solve them and mitigate that risk. And then guess what happens after? The paranoia goes away and you feel good again and everything is at an even greater level of inner peace. You reach monk status after you mitigate everything that could fuck you. Does that make sense?
[33:57] Okay. So, to be clear, this goes for everything. There's literal insurance for this, by the way, where it's called a key person where there's like one person in the business that's responsible for like such a substantial amount of money. You can actually pay an insurance company for a key people insurance where like there actually is again like a literal insurance policy that you can take out against a person in the instance something happens to them. Obviously, it would dramatically affect the revenue of the business.
[34:26] And you can get paid almost like a life insurance policy as a result of that key person insurance. Okay. Yes, there's different conditions. They don't have to always die. Sometimes they could quit or something. It essentially can't be manufactured or false. Of course, it has to like be a legitimate situation, which every insurance policy defines differently. But again, it's like the other way we mitigate key people insurance is just by having our own version of the insurance policy, which is to have more than just one key person. Okay.
[34:57] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Stability - Hooks & Ads Diversification
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de múltiplos hooks e anúncios funcionando, evitar dependência de um único anúncio vencedor, representação de testes constantes e variedade criativa
[35:00] Okay. Outside of the personal profiles, outside of funnels, outside of like all the different people examples I could provide, the other main thing is hooks. And I'm just also going to put the ads under this. How many of you have one ad that just absolutely crushes all your other tests are mediocre in comparison? you have one ad or like one video that just fucking sweeps, dude, and then everything else just sucks ass, you know? Like you try to get other stuff to work, but like it just, you know, doesn't necessarily work all the time.
[35:31] Max says me. So to be clear, it's like Max, that's good pressure for you to work through testing other ad hooks and varants faster than what you are now. And just also just to be clear, ju just based on your condition in which you described yourself since you're new to this group, your messaging's off and that's why you're attracting a bunch of turds. Once you pivot your messaging and make that better, you'll have a tremendously better time with your hooks and you you'll just by that alone, you'll have a much higher ratio of qualified people coming through.
[36:05] You'll also, to be clear, be able to make more money as a result of likely getting rid of your two call close system because right now the only real reason you have a two call close is because you're dealing with a fuckload of turns coming through your funnel. So anyway, moral of the story, I digress.
[36:21] Stability also comes from having multiple hooks, multiple ads that are working well, multiple campaigns that are working well. Okay, I talk about this in this video that I drop here later today where I discuss the two ways that you're spending. So, to be clear, like this whole video is centered around getting to 15K a day in ad spend.
[36:43] I've seen accounts where literally it's like there's one main campaign that is just having so much spend behind it through time that like it results in most of the results of revenue like it is the driver of revenue and if anything happened to that campaign the entire business goes down overnight. The same logic could also be with like you only have one main funnel you know that produces all of the money in the business.
[37:08] I do want to disclose in saying this, okay? I'm not trying to discourage you from pressing the gas and scaling the living fuck out of one main campaign or one main funnel type. Okay? Because at the end of the day, the more money you have, the faster you can typically solve most of these problems anyway that we're talking about.
[37:28] It's like if I, as an example, scale a call funnel to a million dollars a month, okay? And then I also did that through one main campaign or maybe like two main campaigns. Okay. Well, I now have a million dollars a month where I could hire the best people on earth to come in and help me with like a webinar funnel or a challenge funnel or a a I could hire the best salespeople on earth and the best sales management on earth to come in and like help me be able to launch the hydra and like other versions of funnels, simply put that can tack on another million a month.
[38:03] you know, I could hire the best advertisers. Again, that that could help me get away from just having one campaign that works. So, I'm just talking from a perspective of mitigating the risk. It's important to note obviously the risk of having one main funnel type that produces all your revenue and like a handful, meaning like literally one or two campaigns that make all that money is also very risky in that sense.
[38:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Diversification Strategy for 7-Figure Months
NÚMEROS: 5-10 campanhas, 2-3 tipos de funil diferentes, exemplo: $500K + $250K + $250K = $1M
VISUAL: Estrutura de diversificação mostrando múltiplas campanhas e funis, comparação entre concentração vs distribuição de receita, path para meses de 7 dígitos
[38:30] Diversification breeds inner peace when you are in a position of hitting seven figure months and beyond. Okay, the more campaigns you have, ideally it's not an overabundant amount. Generally, as you see here in my list, it's like somewhere between like five and 10 campaigns. Somewhere between, you know, maybe like two to three different funnel types. You know, even if one of the funnels is at like 500K a month and the other two are at 250 and 250, that's still a lot better than just having one main thing.
[39:00] Put put a one in the chat if that makes sense. Unmute yourself if you got a question on that. I want to make sure you all understand this. Again, ones in the chat if you understand what I just said. Unmute yourself if you got any questions on that. Okay, cool.
[39:18] So, yeah, long story short, just to be clear, it's all about inner peace. It's all about preventing getting fucked. Okay, this list goes on and on and on under the stability category. I will name a few more things, but my point being it's like it's relatively different for everybody. You know the risks of your business better than anybody else. And I do encourage that you make a list of what those things are that are technically too risky right now.
[39:44] you know, as an example, just to be clear and write it down, you know, one funnel, you know, one campaign again, one ad account that has all the spend. So, typically, to be clear, like, let me just give some perspective on what we can do with all these accounts here to make sure that we're actually putting spend towards them.
[40:02] As an example, I want to have maybe one account in one business manager that we'll call the main account. That can be where all the direct response for our call funnel go. Then I might have a second account in that same business manager where I spend money on the hammer them strategy. Okay. And then maybe in my second business manager in the main account in my second business manager that's where I have like my webinar funnel ads.
[40:31] Okay. And then in my third business manager, in my main account in my third business manager, that's where I have like the hydra strategy or some other spend. Or maybe I even just take additional spend that I'd otherwise have put into the main account in business manager ad account one and I put it into business manager ad account one, business manager 3 ad account one, you know, instead of just putting it all in one.
[40:55] The point being is like it's a choice to diversify spend instead of just consistently putting it all in the same ad account. And remember, your pixels are shared across all the different business managers anyway. Your page is shared across all the business managers anyway. So, it's not like you're losing anything by diversifying the spend across other accounts. You get what I'm saying? No real risks we're running there. It's not like we're going to see varied performance just based on the account selling. Okay? You get what I'm saying?
[41:26] All right. Feel free to unmute yourself. Does anybody else have anything that they'd consider something that belongs under the stability category?
[41:35] PARTICIPANT: I have a quick question regarding what you just shared, Jeremy. Um, building or setting up this backup business manager. We don't just set them up and keep them hid, but we actually have ad spend running on them.
[41:49] Yeah, of course. Yes.
[41:51] PARTICIPANT: Good. Just wanted to confirm with you.
[41:53] Yeah. All good. Again, anybody unmute yourself. Tell me something else that belongs under the stability column. It's got to be something that we're missing or something that's specific to your business that can be shared. What is it?
[42:06] PARTICIPANT: I mean, I think part of it too is on the fulfillment side, like making sure that your fulfillment capacity can keep up with the marketing as you're starting to really ramp up in scale. Like a lot of times people just have kind of like one warm body CSM that's overloaded or if that CSM is out for a week or something like that or if they're taking two weeks off then like you're not allowed to enroll more clients. So um having like making sure your fulfillment capacity can also expand with your marketing and sales.
[42:37] Makes sense.
[42:39] Cool. Ben, you got something?
[42:41] BEN: Yeah, I was going to say uh having proper legal work with your team. So, making sure they're on NDAAS and just onboarding the right people as well. Making sure there's no flags.
[42:53] You want to go ahead and share your story on that one?
[42:56] BEN: Yeah. Well, yeah. So, I ran my business for a long time. Uh, one of my main businesses is uh it's like a low ticket Discord community and it's like a lot of members. It's a lot of contractors, a lot of staff. So, you know, I started this when I was like really young. So, there's been some like um yeah, there were some like staff members and some contractors with just like like absolutely nothing binding, like just completely free flowing.
[43:25] And then uh yeah, it just like it all went to hell cuz like I had to I fired one well I I tried to preemptively give them the NDA beforehand and they just spazzed out and then uh yeah it just was a fucking show like yeah Ben got absolutely hos tried to fire a key employee. The key employees were all linked up with all the other employees that spread like wildfire. Those key employees then created a competitive product to his product which lost him like a third of his recurring subscriber base all because he didn't already have non-competes and NDAs with the staff upon him.
[44:02] Can you see something as simple as a fucking NDA or non-compete could bring stability?
[44:08] BEN: Yeah, I mean it wasn't that like cataclysmic, but um it definitely was a fucking terrible month. That's for sure.
[44:16] Anything else anybody's got on this?
[44:20] Okay, look, risk mitigation is a choice. Just to be clear, it's like a nuanced, annoying thing to have to do. Okay, it's not the funnest thing in the world. It's not necessarily like, you know, a a singular hour task. You know, like this can take a several hour duration of time just with setting up banks, setting up multiple credit cards and payment methods for the ad accounts.
[44:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Payment Methods & Facebook Invoicing
NÚMEROS: Net 30 payment terms, múltiplos cartões de crédito com limites altos
VISUAL: Estratégia de diversificação de métodos de pagamento, conceito de Facebook invoicing (faturamento), evitar limites de cartão de crédito, operação em net 30
[44:45] Uh, I'll give you another good one as an example under the stability category would be just your payment methods. You know, like you have only AMX's. Uh, I've heard horror stories of AMX will just decide to stop doing business with somebody or AMX will randomly contact you. How many of you have had this happen? MX hits you up, has you submit information and bank statements on your business, and then all of a sudden you officially have a hard limit on your credit cards, whereas prior you did not.
[45:15] You know, I've had that happen to clients before. To be clear, it's like having a high level of spend caps across all your credit cards, having multiple credit cards, you know, again, uh, being able to get approved for Facebook invoicing. Put a one in the chat if you know what Facebook invoicing is. Put a two if you do not. Again, one in the chat if you know what Facebook invoicing is, two if you do not.
[45:40] Okay, we got a bunch of twos already. So, Facebook invoicing gives you the ability to spend the entire month without getting charged anything on a credit card. you accumulate an invoice that they send off at the end of the month and it allows you to operate on a net 30. The net 30 meaning you can pay it off the next month. Okay?
[46:02] And as long as you pay it off by the end of the next month, you're good and you're in good standing with Facebook the entire time. You can then pay that invoice via wire. You can in some instances pay that invoice as well with a high limit credit card. But again, it's like, dude, even if you have uh let's say a multiple-week sales process, okay, so you advertise in the first month and you're not even probable to make your money till the second month, that could also bring a lot of inner peace.
[46:30] Just having Facebook invoicing set up so you don't technically have to pay for the previous month's spend until the next month. You see, operating on a net 30 with Facebook can be huge. Okay? Doesn't always have to just be credit cards, especially when you have limits on your credit cards and you'd probably prefer to just wire anyway. See what I'm saying? That also takes a tremendous amount of risk off you and it puts the risk onto Facebook. God forbid anything happens to your business and you don't make the money back. Does that make sense?
[47:02] Okay. So, to be clear, it's worth Oh, Jelie says, "Can this be set up on any account?" Jolie, you have to qualify for it. So, typically you have to have, you know, a good couple months worth of spend with Facebook and then they'll typically send you an email and say that you're qualified for it. But you can always go into the payments the payment section and see if it's already an option for you to use.
[47:28] U but again if it's not there you can't like request it or anything. You just got to spend consistently for a few months on your on whatever your payment methods currently are. Usually the higher level of spend that you get to the more that you'll see approvals for the Facebook u Facebook invoicing. Okay.
[47:46] Um to be clear again kind of annoying to have to do a lot of this stuff. Totally worth it. Brings you the inner peace for if and when it ever occurs. Treat it like a car accident, right? It's like you don't really give a fuck. You technically like every time you see your car insurance statements for whatever the fuck you're paying them. It's just annoying and it doesn't feel worth it until all of a sudden you get some asshole that runs into you and like you don't have to pay for it, you know?
[48:14] Or you run into somebody else and instead of you having to come out of pocket, they pay for it. You know what I'm saying? It's like that's when it officially becomes worth it. This is the same thing except there's no insurance policy for a lot of this stuff. So, you have to create your own version of these insurance policies. Okay.
[48:32] Okay, if anybody has any follow-up questions, feel free to hit me up about it. Um, to be clear, and just to reiterate, January 18th and 19th is our Q1 inner circle mastermind. Uh, looking forward to seeing everybody there. It'll be a it'll be the first one at the penthouse. Um, tremendous amount of you guys already planning on coming out for it. So, it'll be great networking along with great speakers. I'll announce the speakers probably sometimes towards like later December.
[49:00] And, um, we'll treat it like a little Christmas present for me to you when we announce the speakers list, you know. [laughter] All right. Anybody got anything else in the meantime? Let me know. All right. Good seeing you guys.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$1.2M/mês em ad spend (caso 2019-2020)
$3-4M/mês em receita gerada
$30-40K/dia em spend típico
$700K em receita perdida (5-6 dias de downtime)
$93K retidos pelo PayPal (2017)
$9K/dia em spend (caso recente de domínio bloqueado)
Centenas de milhares perdidos em 7-8 dias
$5K/dia limites típicos em novos ad accounts
25% de capacidade durante weekend de downtime
13% do mês perdido (4 dias de 30)
3 business managers recomendados
9 ad accounts totais (3 por business manager)
3 pixels diferentes instalados simultaneamente
5-10 campanhas para diversificação
2-3 tipos de funil diferentes
Net 30 payment terms com Facebook invoicing
Q1 Inner Circle Mastermind: 18-19 de Janeiro
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Collecting Money:
Múltiplos payment processors (não apenas um)
Condicionar processadores de backup com volume real
Múltiplas contas bancárias (mínimo 2-3)
Evitar PayPal completamente
Distribuir receita entre processadores ativamente
Preparar para auditorias de compliance
Collecting Data:
Sistema "Multiple of Threes": 3 business managers, 3 ad accounts cada
3 pixels únicos instalados em todos os websites
Usar Google Tag Manager para melhor performance
Pixel Hyros como quarto pixel adicional
Condicionar todos os ad accounts com spend real
Proteção contra perda de dados de pixel
Stability:
Personal ad access: perfis de pets como backups
Perfis de funcionários e família no mesmo IP
Funnels duplicados em domínios diferentes ("one in the chamber")
Múltiplos hooks e ads funcionando
5-10 campanhas ativas simultaneamente
2-3 tipos de funil diferentes
Diversificação de closers e equipe
NDAs e non-competes com toda equipe
Capacidade de fulfillment escalável
Múltiplos métodos de pagamento (cartões)
Facebook invoicing para net 30
Distribuir spend entre múltiplos ad accounts
Filosofia Core:
Inner Peace (paz interior)
How can I get fucked (como posso me ferrar)
ESTRUTURA:
Framework de Três Categorias:
Collecting Money - Processadores de pagamento e bancos
Collecting Data - Pixels e business managers
Stability - Profiles, funnels, ads, equipe, métodos de pagamento
Sistema de Backup:
Nunca ter apenas um ponto único de falha
Condicionar backups com uso real, não deixar inativos
Criar "insurance policies" próprias (não existe seguro para ads)
Preparação militar: "ammo in the chamber" sempre pronto
Mentalidade Anti-Reatividade:
Evitar ignorar problemas potenciais
Evitar sedar-se em vez de resolver
Ser proativamente paranóico temporariamente
Alcançar "monk status" após mitigar todos os riscos
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Caso Principal (2019-2020):
Investment: $1.2M/mês em ads
Revenue: $3-4M/mês
Daily Spend: $30-40K
Loss from 5-6 days down: $700K em revenue
Weekend impact: 75% de redução em spend
Caso Recente (Domínio Bloqueado):
Daily Spend: $9K
Downtime: 7-8 dias
Loss: Centenas de milhares em revenue
PayPal Horror Story:
Single Day Revenue: $93K (2017)
Amount Held: $93K
Status: Never recovered
Impact Metrics:
4 dias de downtime = 13% do mês perdido
Weekend + Monday-Wednesday = 5-6 dias típicos de resolução
Backup accounts com $5K/dia limit = 83% de redução em capacidade
ROI de Risk Mitigation:
Cost: Algumas horas de setup tedioso
Benefit: Proteção contra perdas de 6-7 dígitos
Philosophy: Como seguro de carro - só vale quando precisa
DURAÇÃO: 49:00 minutos FORMATO: Group coaching call / Masterclass sobre risk mitigation strategies
